97. We also recommended that the farmer beneficiaries be required to return the excess grant of capital assistance.
observations.managementAction:
According to Management, it was not possible to procure the cattle through public bidding because the Municipality had a previous experience where the cattle acquired and given to the farmers were of inferior quality. They mentioned that the Department of Agriculture is aware that there is no qualified supplier in the province thus the method of releases was converted to giving cash to farmer beneficiaries for them to personally buy cattle on their own.
The amount loaned included the purchase price of each cattle with the remaining used for feeds and other needs in cattle fattening. Management mentioned that the beneficiaries did not fail to pay their obligation so a next batch of beneficiaries could avail of the loan.

observations.recommendation: 98. We recommended that the Municipality close its deposit accounts with the PVB and RBB and deposit the same to a government financial institution granting high interest rates.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipality was in the process of closing its PVB account since it has been tagged as dormant.
The Municipality still needed to close RBBI.

observations.recommendation: 99. We recommended that the Municipality procure common-use supplies from PS-DBM and, henceforth, comply with the provisions of Administrative Order No. 17 dated July 28, 2011 and DBM Circular Letter No. 2011-6 dated August 25, 2011.
observations.managementAction: Management had resorted to Shopping to procure common-use supplies and might consider procuring from the depot if it is no longer impractical due to distance, cost, and time constraints. The LGU had noted that the supplies in PS_DBM depot were of inferior quality or in stock for a long time due to their physical appearance.

observations.recommendation: 100. We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee strictly observed Section 52.1 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 in the procurement of goods.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality still needed to evaluate the transactions for the year.

observations.recommendation: 101. We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Treasurer require the taxpayers to submit an Income Statement/Statement of Receipts and Expenditures when applying for renewal of business taxes to ascertain the accuracy of the declared gross receipts subject to tax. If the examination of the books could not be conducted immediately, the taxpayers may be required to submit their preceding year’s financial statements as temporary bases in computing the business taxes.
observations.managementAction: Taxpayers were required to submit proof of income or gross receipts when applying for renewal of business permits to compute the tax due, but due to a lack of manpower, not all business taxpayers were examined. Only bigger business establishments were examined. The smaller business establishments, on the other hand, were only interviewed and required to fill out forms to show their Income /Receipts and Expenditures.

observations.recommendation: 103. We recommended submission of the revised Solid Waste Management Plan following the Annotated Outline prescribed by the National Solid Waste Management Commission and submit the same to the National Solid Waste Management Commission for approval pursuant to Section 16 of RA No. 9003 for an effective solid waste collection and disposal.
observations.managementAction: The amended SWM had already been submitted. The SWM had been returned by the National Solid Waste Management Commission for corrections.

observations.recommendation:
104. The Mayor to strictly require the Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of properties and reconcile the same with related property and accounting records and for the municipal treasurer’s office to maintain property cards in accordance with existing regulations.
105. The Accountant to reclassify the fully depreciated and unserviceable assets from the Property Plant and Equipment to Other Asset account and to look into the disbursement vouchers and other documents pertaining to the unbooked assets and record the same in the book of accounts.
106. The Municipal Treasurer to locate the missing hand held radio issued to the former barangay captain of Barangay Calangag and the Iwata Time Recorder amounting to ₱24,642.50 and ₱14,950.00, respectively. Also, locate the engine previously installed in the LGU owned patrol boat and get back the 70CC, Econo, power Honda kept in a private residence and henceforth, refrain from storing government properties in a private residence/place.
observations.managementAction: Management maintained that the items had yet to be located. The Municipality is gearing towards digitalization which will ease the reconciliation of the total collectibles from the Basic and Special Education Tax with the balances of the Receivables. The Subsidiary Ledgers will also be maintained on this per barangay. The items had yet to be located.

observations.recommendation:
110. Conduct a Physical Inventory of all involved properties to determine the existence and physical condition of these assets.
111. Settle the Due to NGAS account by submitting Liquidation Reports to the grantors so that the latter’s books are also adjusted accordingly, before effecting the transfer to the General Fund.
112. Based on the physical inventory, require that properties, plant and equipment under the trust fund be immediately transferred to the specific account of general fund for proper presentation of the financial statements pursuant to Section 104 of NGAS.

The accountable officer with an unliquidated cash advance was no longer in service due to a transfer. The accountable officer with overdue cash advances did not have their salary withheld but liquidated only after the due date. There were still some cash advances that were overdue for liquidation as of year-end. The accountable officer with an unliquidated cash advance was no longer in service due to a transfer.
The Municipality still needs to implement full settlement and recovery actions.

observations.recommendation:
110. Conduct a Physical Inventory of all involved properties to determine the existence and physical condition of these assets.
111. Settle the Due to NGAS account by submitting Liquidation Reports to the grantors so that the latter’s books are also adjusted accordingly, before effecting the transfer to the General Fund.

observations.managementAction:

Physical Inventory Count had yet to be conducted. Settle the Due to NGAS account by submitting Liquidation Reports to the grantors so that the latter’s books are also adjusted accordingly. After the Physical Inventory, the transfer of the booking from the Trust Fund to the General Fund shall be effected. The Municipality had already maintained the PPE Ledger Cards. Due to the lack of physical inventory count, there may be unidentified PPEs with no ledger cards.

observations.recommendation:
114. Members of the Bids and Awards Committee/ Technical Working Group should allow only eligible prospective bidders to participate in the public bidding for the procurement of Goods and Infrastructure Project.
115. The BAC allow only eligible prospective bidders to participate in the public bidding for the procurement of goods and services.
116. In the post qualification, the BAC/TWG should verify, validate, and ascertain the genuineness, validity and accuracy of the legal, technical and financial documents submitted by the bidder with the Local Chief Bids (LCB) before awarding the same.
117. Personnel preparing the purchase orders should strictly indicate the basic data on the PO pursuant to Section B.1 of COA Circular 96-010 dated August 15, 1996.
118. The agency should submit the lacking documents pursuant to Section 138 (f) of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual, Volume 1.
observations.managementAction:
Future procurements still have to be continuously validated. Some POs still lacked basic information in CY 2023.
Lacking documents have yet to be submitted.

observations.recommendation: 119. We recommended submission of the monthly trial balances and financial statements within the reglementary period in accordance with Section 70 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, otherwise, we shall be constrained to withhold the salary of concerned officials and implement other sanctions and penalties as provided for under our laws.
observations.managementAction: Reports were not submitted within the reglementary period. The Municipality is gearing towards digitalization and is aiming be able to generate financial reports within the reglementary period by then.

observations.recommendation: 120. Required the Municipal Accountant to assign specific personnel to prepare and submit the monthly bank reconciliation statements to COA and the depository bank so that timely verification and review can be made and discrepancies immediately identified and corrected.
observations.managementAction: The bank reconciliation for the PVB bank account was not prepared due to its dormant nature so there were no transactions affecting it. Management is planning to close the PVB account within the year.

observations.recommendation:
121. Consider increasing rental rates or implement measures to reduce operating costs of the subject municipality’s economic enterprises, and require the municipal treasurer to accelerate the collection of unpaid rentals by sending bills of collection to delinquent stall or space lessees, invoking the sanctions provided under the Contract of Lease to make the collection more effective.
In addition, look for additional revenue sources, such as charging minimal fees for the use of the market’s comfort rooms.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality had already drafted its revised revenue code for review and approval.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive
require the (a) Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to conduct a regular
reconciliation of balances under the Cash in Bank account, exert best effort to validate and
reconcile balances between cashbooks and ledgers, and make necessary adjustments once
reconciling items have been verified and substantiated; and (b) Municipal Accountant to submit the
monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) for verification within the prescribed period provided
by COA Circular No. 96-011.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE), in coordination with the
Municipal Accountant, to: (a) ensure that a cash advance is granted only after the
liquidation of the previous one by requiring certification from the latter confirming its
settlement/accounting before releasing the new one; and (b) ensure strict
adherence to applicable rules and regulations governing cash advances.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive
instruct the (a) Municipal Accountant to consistently recognize the purchased
inventories to their respective asset accounts and practice proper derecognition
thereof; and (b) MGSO and MAO to ensure coordination between their offices for
the reconciliation and timely recording of receipts and issuances of inventories.

We reiterated our recommendation, with modification, that the Local Chief
Executive (LCE) require the (a) Inventory Team to (i) conduct complete annual
physical inventory-taking of PPE; (ii) submit a complete RPCPPE not later than
January 31 of each year; and (iii) avail the one-time cleansing of PPEs in accordance
with COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We also reiterated that the LCE designate a dedicated property officer, if the
budget allows, to ensure that all functions pertaining to supply and property
management are carried out properly.

We reiterated that the Management (a) exhaust all possible means to secure
copies of the TCT of the lots and buildings owned by the Municipality and submit to
the Audit Team for verification; and (b) designate personnel to work on
documentation for the legality of ownership of the lots together with the structures
built thereon; and (c) appropriate additional funds to cover the cost of transfer of
titles.

We reiterated, with modification, our prior year’s audit recommendations
that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal General Services Officer to (a)
prepare the IIRUP to include all unserviceable properties to facilitate the proper
disposal of the unserviceable properties; and (b) create a Disposal and Appraisal
Committee which will undertake the necessary actions for the immediate disposal of
the said properties pursuant to Section 79 of P.D. 1445.

We commended Management for its efforts and notable compliance with
pertinent regulations in managing the 20% development fund. However, we
recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive require the
concerned offices to review and enhance the Municipality’s planning and budgeting
strategies to ensure complete implementation of budgeted projects.

We commended the Municipality and MENRO for its efforts as recognized by
DILG and Geocycle Philippines for its responsiveness to the environment awareness
cause. However, we still recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief
Executive and the Solid Waste Management Board (a) enforce the proper wastes
segregation at source to reap the benefits of the “No Segregation No Collection
Policy” instead of having mixed waste materials left strewn in public places; (b)
ensure that wastes in the Market Transfer Station are timely collected and
transported to the Municipal MRF and observe cleanliness in its surrounding; (c)
provide each barangay with the needed garbage truck, subject to availability of
funds, and facilitate the establishment of MRFs in barangay-level in accordance with
its 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan for the period of 2022-2031.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive Officer direct the (a)
MDRRMC to include under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities
charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous year in the LDRRMFIP; and (b)
Municipal Accountant to maintain Subsidiary Ledgers for transfers of agency’s
unutilized DRRMF to the special trust fund by year of transfer in accordance with
the provisions of COA Circular 2012-002.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive Officer
direct the MDRRMC to (i) monitor the implementation of the LDRRMP to optimize
the utilization of its LDRRMF in order to lessen the socio-economic and
environmental impacts of disasters in the community and disaster risks, including
projected climate risks, (ii) submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF
on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the LDRRMC and
Local Development Council (LDC) to the COA auditor of the LGU as mandated in
COA Circular 2012-002.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal
Accountant to (i) record purchase of welfare/relief goods and other expendable items
to inventory account and issuances thereof to the appropriate expense accounts; and
(ii) maintain corresponding Subsidiary Ledgers for each type of Disaster Response
and Rescue Equipment set forth under COA Circular 2014-002 and COA Circular
2012-002, respectively; and (iii) to record the ₱2,000,000.00 FA received in a separate
SL of the Trust Liability account and utilize the said NDRRMF in accordance with
the Memorandum of Agreement.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the Municipal
Treasurer to conduct random examination of the business taxpayers’ books of
accounts to ascertain the correctness and accuracy of assessments of business tax.

We also recommended and Management agreed that the LCE require the
Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to investigate the
variance noted between the RPT/SET Receivables accounts and the RPT delinquency
report and make necessary adjustments, if necessary, to present fairly the accounts
in the financial statements.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Local Chief Executive (a) direct
the Municipal Accountant and the various Departmental Heads to coordinate and
strictly observe the timely preparation and submission of the Financial Reports, DVs
and their supporting documents to the Audit Team within the reglementary period
prescribed under the aforementioned Circulars; (b) instruct the Municipal
Accountant to assess if additional workforce would be necessary to facilitate the
prompt submission of the documents to the Office of the Auditor.

However, we recommended and Management agreed that the Local Executive
Chief instruct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the
General Ledgers to update the book balances and to continually monitor the accounts
to avoid mistakes and reflect the correct amounts of withholding and remittances
made to the BIR, GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG, thereby providing accurate
information to readers.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the accountable officers to
immediately liquidate his/her outstanding cash advances as prescribed in COA
Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to refrain from
granting additional cash advances to any official or employee without liquidating
first her prior cash advances, and to designate instead additional SDOs for the grant
of additional cash advances for special activities/projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and City Accountant
to:
a. require AOs with outstanding cash advances for field/activity operating
expenses to prepare Report of Disbursements and submit the same within 5 days
after the end of each month;
b. require AOs to return or refund cash advances that are no longer needed or has
not been used for a period of two months;
c. ensure that cash advances granted did not exceed the two-month requirement
prescribed by Item 4.4.2 of COA Circular 97-002;
d. ensure that payrolls are sufficiently provided with dates of payouts; and
e. propose specific and achievable measures to resolve delays in the liquidation of
cash advances, which include, among others, the OIC - City Accountant’s regular
sending of demand letters to AOs with a reminder that their salaries could be
withheld should they still fail to liquidate after demand.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to record the
collection of excess CAs on the SLs on the actual dates of refund or receipt to ensure
reliable information posted in the SL.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Human Resource Development
and Management Office (HRDMO), in coordination with the Department Heads of the
City, to ensure that the hiring of JO/COS workers are supported with contracts whereby
the workers’ job descriptions, duties and expected deliverables from them are clearly
provided or defined and against which their performance and accomplishments, and
the necessity of their hiring and reasonableness of their payments would be evaluated.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CGSO to cause the insurance of the
government motor vehicles against any insurable risk and pay the premiums
consistent with the provisions of RA No. 656.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to recognize the
registration of vehicles with the LTO to the appropriate expense account instead of the
Insurance Expense as provided under the Revised Chart of Accounts for (LGUs,
Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the City Mayor require the City
Treasurer and the City Accountant to jointly evaluate the impairment of the receivables
and to comply with the documentary requirements and guidelines prescribed under COA
Circular No. 2016-005 to facilitate the submission to the COA of the request for
authority to write-off the dormant receivables amounting to P1,756,156.60 or to avail of
the one-time cleansing of dormant receivables within one year from the effectivity of
COA Circular No. 2023-008.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and City Treasurer to
continue their ongoing efforts to determine the origin and details of the reported
discrepancy between the reciprocal accounts Due to and Due from Other Funds.

We recommended that the City Mayor, through the Head of the HRDMO, expedite the
hiring and/or promotion of qualified personnel to reduce if not totally eliminate the need
to appoint casual and JO workers, thus ensuring a more efficient and effective
government service thru strategic management of human resources.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Officer (CDRRMO) to submit the distribution lists duly signed by the
actual recipients/beneficiaries and pictures showing actual distribution for validation of
the Audit Team.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the CDRRMO and City Budget Officer to
refrain from obligating and charging LDRRMF for expenditures which are not directly
related to the implementation of disaster risk management activities, otherwise the
expenses shall be disallowed in audit for being contrary to NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC
No. 2013-1.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City DRRM Officer to submit the
necessary pertinent documents to establish the inclusion of the subject volunteers in
the official list of ACDVs, as required under NDRRMC MC No. 64 s, 2021, and written
justification on why a total of 89 regular, casual or job order employees were included
in the group insurance, otherwise the expenses shall be disallowed in audit for being
contrary to RA No. 10121.

We recommended and Management agreed to prioritize the implementation of
the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts by strictly following the guidelines and
procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, to
have reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition, and
accountability.

We recommended that Management close their checking account with LBP
and return the remaining balance of the Trust Fund to the DILG to avoid
incurring future dormancy fees on the dormant bank account.

We recommended that Management strictly observe the provisions of R.A. No.
7305 and its revised IRR for the grant of hazard pay to its PHWs. We further
recommended that Management secure the documentary requirements for the
payment of hazard pay to the PHWs as listed under COA Circular No. 2012-
001 dated June 14, 2012.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor appoint or
designate an additional DO to avoid granting multiple cash advances to the
incumbent DO.

We also recommended that no additional cash advances be granted to the DO
unless the previous cash advance is settled or properly accounted for.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management ensure that cash
advances granted to the DO Designate do not exceed the maximum cash
accountabilities covered by his fidelity bond, otherwise, effect the increase of
his fidelity bond commensurate with his accountabilities, in accordance with
the Bureau of Treasury Circular No. 02-2019 dated April 25, 2019, to protect
the interests of the Municipal government.

We recommended that the HRMO-Designate ensure that the accuracy,
authenticity, and completeness of all the requirements and supporting papers in
connection with all cases of appointments are thoroughly reviewed and checked
before submitting the same to the CSC to avoid disapproval/invalidation of
appointments.

We recommended that Management review the Municipality’s functions,
systems and procedures, organizational structure, and staffing pattern to
address any needs and gaps in the human resource complement.

We also recommended that Management comply with all the provisions of the
JC and consider existing qualified COS/JO workers when filling all its vacant
plantilla positions, and provide for wages of COs/JOs at a rate equivalent to a
comparable position in the government plus premium if funds are available.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Planning and
Development Office and the Municipal Planning and Development Council to
include in the Annual Investment Plan and eventually in the Municipality’s
proposed budget only those programs and projects that are “Implementation-
Ready”.

We also recommended that Management address the problem of the funded
projects that have become unimplementable due to changes in events or
conditions such as price changes or site conditions, among others, Management
review its projects funded under the continuing appropriation over the last
three years or any timeframe as they deem appropriate, to ascertain if the
projects are still viable for implementation at this time. Otherwise, consider
their reversion, in accordance with Section 322 of RA No. 7160, to release
funds and make them available for other meaningful uses.

We recommended that Management endeavor to implement the projects
during the budget year and become proactive in utilizing the LDRRMF for
PPAs that will eliminate flood risks, lessen the adverse impacts of hazards and
calamities, or prepare and train residents on how to appropriately respond to
any disasters that may happen.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant take up an accounting entry
in the General Fund (GF) and the Trust Fund (TF) books to record the
obligation of the unexpended QRF and the authorized receipt of funds from
the GF, respectively, for the transfer of the unexpended balance of the QRF at
the end of the year using the accounts discussed in the foregoing paragraphs
in compliance with the accounting requirements under Annex C of COA
Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and COA Circular No. 2015-
009 dated December 1, 2015, to fairly present the accounts in the financial
statements.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Development Council (LDC) and the Municipal Planning and Development Council as the LDC secretariat prepare an approved Supplemental or Amended AIP for newly identified and qualified PPAs that will be sourced out of the 20% DF to be submitted to the Sangguniang Bayan for adoption and approval.
observations.managementAction: The Management assured the Audit Team to observe compliance with the recommendations of strictly observing revision of Annual Investment Plan (AIP) for the re-programmed Projects, Programs and Activities (PPAs).

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to (a) require the Property Office to renew all PARs that reached the prescribed period of three years and come up with a systematic filing of the documents preferably in alphabetical sequence for easy access and reference; and (b) indicate in the PAR any subsequent transfer of the asset from one accountable officer to another.
observations.managementAction: The Management agreed to adhere to the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to properly account for the stale checks in accordance with the provisions of Section 59 of NGAS Manual, Volume I to reflect the correct balance of the Cash in Bank account.
observations.managementAction: The Management agreed to adhere to the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that the Chairman and the LSB members (a) continue to utilize the Special Education Fund for priority projects and for purposes explicitly allowed in the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular Nos. 1 and 2, s. 2020 in addition to Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017; (b) be more specific in planning for the PPAs to be implemented and coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to address the inconsistencies found in their reports and records.

We also recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant (a) observe the proper charging and reclassification of accounts in accordance with the nature of transactions; and (b) make the necessary adjustment and/or reclassification of accounts in the books.
observations.managementAction: The Management together with the Chairman and LSB members agreed to adhere to the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive (a) ensure that qualifications of the BAC and TWG members are considered in accordance with applicable laws and regulations; (b) direct the Accountant to compute the payment of honoraria of procurement officials in accordance with Budget Circular No. 2004-5A, as amended by BC No. 2007-2; (c) submit the required documents mentioned in Item 5.7.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and (d) ensure that all future claims of honoraria are computed and granted in accordance with the applicable laws, rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The Management agreed to adhere to the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive allow the BAC Chairperson to designate the members of the BAC-TWG in adherence to Section 12.1 prescribed in the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Chairperson agreed to designate the members of the BAC-TWG in adherence to Section 12.1 prescribed in the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive (a) facilitate the transfer of the inmates to the Municipal prison cell inasmuch as the threat on the spread of COVID-19 has already been controlled; (b) request the PNP Head at the Municipality to deploy the PNP personnel to the different Police Stations within the Municipality; and (c) instruct the Municipal Treasurer to investigate and/or look into the cause of high electric bills to avoid the incurrence of unnecessary expenses.
observations.managementAction: The Management agreed to adhere to the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We once again recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive can maintain updated (a) instruct the GFPS to set up, monitor and maintain appropriate systems and mechanisms that would ensure the generation, processing, review, and updating of sex-aggregated data or GAD database to serve as basis in the performance-based and gender- responsive planning and budgeting; and (b) ensure critical assessment and planning of GAD-related PPAs to allocate proper amounts that will help achieve the set objectives whilst maximizing the use of the fund.
observations.managementAction: The Management adheres to the recommendations of the Audit Team and noted the following: • The CBMS was not yet in place due to various reasons such as absence of funds, lack of equipment and personnel. • The GFPS, through MPDO and MSWDO maintains data using the System developed by DSWD Central Office. The System can generate data by barangay and statistics by year. • The concerned departments shall exert effort to attain a 100% implementation of their respective PPAs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and City Accountant to: a. propose measures to ensure that cash advances are granted within reasonable period prior to its use; b. ensure that cash advances granted for cash for work program are based on approved payrolls; c. require AOs with outstanding cash advances for field/activity operating expenses to prepare Report of Disbursements and submit the same within 5 days after the end of each month; d. require AOs to return or refund cash advances that are no longer needed or has not been used for a period of two months; e. ensure that cash advances granted did not exceed the two-month requirement prescribed by Item 4.4.2 of COA Circular 97-002; f. ensure that payrolls are sufficiently provided with dates of payouts; and g. propose specific and achievable measures to resolve delays in the liquidation of cash advances, which include, among others, the City Accountant’s regular sending of demand letters to AOs with a reminder that their salaries could be withheld should they still fail to liquidate after demand.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to ensure that accomplishment reports and approved DTRs of JO personnel are attached to the liquidation reports of cash advances for wages of JO personnel to avoid audit suspension of their wages. Moreover, instruct the authority of the City Treasurer to require claimants of wages other than the JO personnel to present authorization letters and proof of identifications and include the same as part of the supporting documents in the liquidation reports.
observations.managementAction: The Management instructed the City Treasurer to require the claimants to present authorization letters and identification documents and include the same as part of the supporting documents in the liquidation reports. The Management also required the uploading of DTRs, accomplishment reports and other supporting documents as part of the liquidation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer and the Management Information System Department Head to coordinate with the computerized system provider to review the computation and recommend necessary upgrade or improvement thereon to address the issue on computation and posting of tax adjustments.
observations.managementAction: The Management has coordinated with the computerized system provider to address the computation and posting of tax adjustments. The system was updated by the system provider accordingly.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the implementing units of the City to prepare and submit their respective PPMPs prior to the formulation of CY 2023 APP.
observations.managementAction: All units were strictly required to submit their respective PPMPs before the formulation of CY 2023 APP.

observations.recommendation:
8. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the recorded cash advances from the asset account Advances for Operating Expenses (1-03-05-010) to the appropriate account Advances to Special Disbursing Officer (1-03-05-030) by making the following adjusting entry:
Debit Credit
Advances to Special Disbursing Officer 2,880,000.00
Advances for Operating Expenses 2,880,000.00
9. We further recommended that, henceforth, the Municipal Accountant ensure the corresponding liquidation of the cash advances that were previously credited to the account Advances for Operating Expenses (1-03-05-010) be rectified.
10. We furthermore recommended that Management refer to the description of accounts as a guide in recording expenses in the books of accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015, to avoid erroneous entries.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant had already recorded the cash advances appropriately in CY 2023.
The books for CY 2022 were already closed thus no adjustment was made. Management had already recorded the cash advances appropriately.

observations.recommendation:
15. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the lacking supporting documents as required under Sections 9.1 to 9.1.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to enable the Audit Team to properly evaluate the accuracy and validity of the subject disbursement.
15. We further recommended that, henceforth, the Municipal Accountant ensure the completeness of the supporting documents for every transaction of the Municipality before payment is made in compliance with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipality had already submitted the lacking supporting documents for the subject project. Some transactions during the year still lacked some documentary requirements.
The Municipal Accountant submitted the lacking supporting documents to the Auditor during the exit conference.

observations.recommendation:
23. We recommended that Management install internal control mechanisms by using the RIS to request and issue supplies and materials carried in stock and to consolidate the same in the SSMI, which shall be submitted to the Municipal Accountant for recording to the appropriate expense accounts for the supplies consumed.
24. Further, we recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to stop the practice of recording purchases of supplies and materials directly to expense accounts and henceforth, to record purchases and utilization of supplies and materials in accordance with Section 114 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I and Section IV of COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014, to fairly present the expense and inventory accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction:
Perusal of the DVs showed that not all had an RIS attached while others were attached with an incomplete RIS.
Management had already stopped recording supplies directly to expenses.

observations.recommendation: 33. We recommended that the MDRRM and MDRRMO review and update annually the MDRRMFIP to include prior years’ unexpended LDRRMF in accordance with Sections 5.1.2 and 5.1.15 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, in order to enhance disaster/calamity preparedness and response in view of the availability of more funds.
observations.managementAction: The prior year unexpended LDRRMF is already included in the MDRRMFIP.

observations.recommendation:
43. We recommended that Management require the Bids and Awards Committee to submit justification for the procurement of the items above the highest suggested retail price of the DOH.
44. We further recommended that Management procure medical supplies based on the SRP set by appropriate government agencies to ensure that the Municipality get the most advantageous prices.
observations.managementAction:
The BAC Chairman had submitted Bids and Awards Committee Awards and a justification stating their observation that during the pandemic, the demand for medical supplies was high but the supply was low. Management justified their purchases by deciding that purchasing at the higher prices was more practical as people’s lives were at risk. Management believes that DOH should look into the suppliers violating the SRP.
Per checking with the CY 2023 acquisitions of medical supplies, some items had been procured at prices higher than the DOH suggested retail price.

observations.recommendation:
45. We recommended that Management submit the following documents be immediately submitted to the Auditor to determine compliance with the conditions on the grant of COVID-19 Hazard Pay set forth in Paragraph 4 of DBM Budget Circular No. 2020-01 dated March 24, 2020, to wit:
a. Authority for the entitlement of the allowance; and
b. IATF Resolution of the Risk Certification in the workplace
46. Likewise, we recommended that Management submit copy of the approved contracts of personnel under COS and JO scheme.
observations.managementAction:
Management had already submitted the authority from the head of agency/ requiring employees and workers to physically report to work during the ECQ, as well as the IATF Resolution certifying the risks in the workplace.
The approved contracts of personnel under the COS and JO schemes had not yet been submitted.

observations.recommendation:
47. Management establish a procedure in identifying PHWs who shall be exposed to high risk or low risk hazards including the duration of such exposure duly approved by the Secretary of Health to support claims for hazard pay; and
48. The Municipal Accountant require all the PHWs to support their claims for hazard pay with proof of actual exposure to specific health hazards and occupational risks with certification from appropriate agencies and other documents required under Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
observations.managementAction:
Management had already submitted a certification from the DOH. The need to assess high or low-risk hazards is no longer necessary.
Management had not yet implemented the requirement to submit all documentary requirements under Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 as the LGU had not yet received the certification from the DOH.

observations.recommendation: 68. We recommended that, henceforth, the Local Chief Executive direct all department heads concerned to submit their respective duly accomplished AAPSI and assign personnel to consolidate the same for submission to the Auditor within 60 days upon receipt of the AAR for the succeeding years.
observations.managementAction: The AAPSI had been submitted within 60 days from the receipt of the AAR on August 25, 2023.

observations.recommendation:
85. We recommended that Management prioritize the establishment and operation of a sanitary landfill in accordance with Sections 40 and 41 of RA No. 9003 and meantime, take action to convert the existing open dump site into a controlled dump and hasten its closure within a specific time frame.
86. We further recommended that the municipal ordinance on solid waste management be strictly enforced and all barangays be involved in the implementation of waste reduction and segregation at source in accordance with RA No. 9003.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality entered into a Memorandum of Agreement with the City of Bayawan for the disposal of residual waste effective April 1, 2021. Management imposed upon the residents of the Municipality to compost their bio-degradable wastes. Only residual wastes were collected and deposited in the dumpsite.

observations.recommendation:
87. We recommended that the MDRRM Council include in its LDRRMFIP the PPAs to be funded by the unexpended LDRRMF.
88. We further recommended that the LDRRM Officer submit the Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRM on or before the 15th day after the end of each month to the Office of the Auditor pursuant to Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
observations.managementAction:
The PPAs to be funded from the unexpended LDRRMF had already been included in the MDRRMFIP.
Reports were prepared by the Municipality but not submitted to the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: 93. We recommended that the Municipality insure the project with the General Insurance Fund of the General Service Insurance System (GSIS) in order that the government may be indemnified from any damage to or loss of the project in case of fire, earthquake, storm or other calamity.
observations.managementAction: Management had inquired with the GSIS regarding the insurance coverage for the projects funded under the BUB. According to the GSIS, they only accept the Contractor’s All-Risks Insurance while the project is still ongoing. Management had resorted to this practice.

observations.recommendation: 122. Install government plates to the three municipal vehicles which bear private plates and mark all municipal vehicles with the words for “OFFICIAL USE ONLY” with the name of the municipality underneath, and instruct the treasurer and accountant to desist from paying expenses for the maintenance of these vehicles unless the requirements under COA Circular No. 75-6 are complied.
observations.managementAction:
The vehicles still bore private plates but are currently unserviceable so the Municipality is no longer paying for the maintenance of these vehicles.
The vehicles still bore private plates but are currently unserviceable so the Municipality is no longer paying for the maintenance of these vehicles.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous recommendations that the:
a. Municipal Accountant (i) effect the adjustment of the identified
reconciling items totaling ₱4,219,934.11 in the books of accounts and
provide the supporting documents justifying the discrepancy reported in
the BRS and CIB-LCCA account balances; (ii) take up the necessary
correcting entries to record the ₱20,000.00 initial deposit for LBP-EPS to
appropriate cash account; and (iii) cause the preparation and immediate
submission of the monthly BRS for the DBP-HDMF (51048-3605-5),
LBP-EPS (0502-1178-15 and LBP-BCC accounts and ensure timely
preparation and submission of monthly BRS, in accordance with the
guidelines prescribed under COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2,
1996; and
b. Municipal Treasurer make representation with the Bureau of Internal
Revenue (BIR) to facilitate the prompt processing of the refund or other
recourse which may be deemed appropriate, for the noted over
remittance of tax amounting to ₱73,212.97.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation to ensure strict
compliance with the procedural guidelines on the receipt and issuance of
inventory items prescribed in Chapter 7 of Manual on the NGAS for LGUs,
Volume I, and further recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Inventory Team conduct physical inventory of all its inventory
items and prepare and submit the RPCI not later than July 31 and
January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively in
compliance with Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs,
Volume I;
b. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Inventory Committee (i)
reconcile the balances of the inventory per books with the inventory
reports, (ii) verify and analyze the discrepancies, and (iii) prepare
adjustments to the affected records, if any;
c. Municipal Accountant submit to Audit Team the lacking distribution list
of welfare goods and foods supplies per barangay and or individuals to
substantiate their issuances and to ensure proper recording of financial
transactions in accordance with the revised chart of accounts prescribed
under COA Circular 2015-009 dated December 01, 2015; and
d. On receipt and issuance of donated drugs and medicines, the concerned
offices furnish the Municipal Accountant and MGSO - Designee copies of
pertinent Deed of Donations, Memorandum of Agreements (MOA), RIS,
distribution list and any other required documents for proper recording
thereof in the books of accounts and stock cards.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Inventory Team to formulate plans and strategize the conduct of physical
count of all its PPE and prepare and submit the RPCPPE not later than
January 31 of the ensuing year in compliance with Section 124 of the
Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
b. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Inventory Committee to (i)
reconcile the balances of the PPE per books with the inventory reports,
(ii) verify and analyze the discrepancies, (iii) prepare adjustments to the
affected records, and (iv) avail of the one-time cleansing of accounts per
COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020;
c. The Municipal Accountant to (i) re-compute and record depreciation for
CY 2021 completed infrastructure projects and prepare appropriate
adjusting entries to correct the understated computation of depreciation,
(ii) exert effort to identify the details and status of the subject PPE costing
₱10,819,528.01 for the computation of their depreciation, and deemed
completed projects still recorded under the CIP accounts totaling
₱6,066,215.79 for proper reclassification to appropriate PPE accounts,
and (iii) prepare appropriate adjusting entries to reclassify issued semi-
expendable items totaling ₱227,565.00 from PPE accounts to Government
Equity account; and
d. Municipal General Service Officer - Designee prepare and submit the
IIRUP and other required documents in accordance with the Manual on
PPE Appraisal and Disposal to facilitate the disposal of the noted
unserviceable government properties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. concerned personnel to (i) prepare and submit to GIF GSIS the PIF to
facilitate the assessment of the premium due by the GSIS underwriting
official for all serviceable Municipal properties, including the 12 vehicles
and heavy equipment cited above; and (ii) ensure that all Municipal
properties are adequately covered and insured with the General
Insurance Fund of GSIS in accordance with Republic Act No. 656, as
amended by Presidential Decree No. 245 or the Property Insurance Law
and DILG MC No. 2016-63; and
b. Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the rules and regulations on
future recording of prepayments such as insurance premiums in
compliance with Section 64 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with
the BAC Chairperson and concerned offices cause the submission to the Audit
Team of the lacking Mayor’s Permit, Income Tax Return, and Omnibus Sworn
Statement (OSS) for the payments for antigen rapid test kits and the distribution
list of food items and multivitamins, for further evaluation.

We recommended the Management to:
a. provide written comments and or justification (i) on the deficiencies noted
on the acceptance and payments of laptops whose specifications were
under par or not in conformance with the POs; (ii) on why brand names
of requested items totaling ₱127,860.00 were included in the PRs; and (iii)
on why the BAC resorted to the use of shopping modality despite the
items were not readily available off-the-shelf, contrary to Section 118,
Chapter 7 of the Manual on the NGAS, Volume I and the above cited
provisions of the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184, respectively;
b. require the respective suppliers to replace and/or upgrade the delivered
ICT equipment to conform with the specifications agreed in the PO,
otherwise, the cost deficiency shall be determined and refunded, as may
be deemed appropriate;
c. direct the Inspectorate Team to verify and ensure that delivered items
conform to the quantity and specifications stated in the approved PO and
the Delivery Receipt, before signing the “Inspection” column in the AIR;
d. remind the requisitioning offices not to include brand names of items in
the PRs, in compliance with Section 18 of the 2016 RIRR of RA 9184, to
ensure the most advantageous price of items to be procured will be
availed by the Municipality; and
e. require the BAC to ensure that PO specifies the brand names and the
complete specifications of the items quoted by the supplier in response to
the advertised relevant characteristics and/or performance requirements
of the items needed by the Municipality, to serve as reference during
deliveries and inspection in adherence with COA Circular No. 96-010
dated August 15, 1996.

We recommended that Management:
a. provide written comments and/or justification on deficiencies noted,
particularly (i) the technical specifications of 17 multi-purpose/rescue
vehicles were seemed tailored-fit with that of Isuzu Traviz, (ii) the
technical specifications of items required to be fitted/provided to 16 out of
17 vehicles were not indicated in the PR and bidding documents, and (iii)
the name and logo of the Municipal Mayor and the name of concerned
Punong Barangays were included on stickers on both sides of the multi-
purpose vehicles issued to BHERT of different Barangays, MDRRMO
and BCC; and
b. require the concerned municipal officials and Punong Barangays to cause
the immediate removal of unnecessary logos and names of government
officials to the cited government vehicles in compliance with the above
cited DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2010-101 and COA Circular Nos.
2013-004 and 2012-003.

We recommended that Management provide written comments and or
justification on:
a. why were (i) the technical specifications of three (3) dump trucks were
tailor fitted with a particular model that Isuzu Motors are
manufacturing, contrary to the above cited provisions of the 2016 RIRR
of RA No. 9184, (ii) the acceptance and inspection of two (2) dump trucks
were made before the equipment were actually delivered inconsistent
with the rules and regulations under Section 118, Chapter 7 of MNGAS,
Volume I for LGUs, and (iii) the name of the Municipal Mayor was
included on stickers on both sides of the dump/garbage trucks; and
b. require the concerned municipal officials to (i) strictly observe the rules
and regulations under the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184 and Section 118,
Chapter 7 of MNGAS, Volume I to ensure regularity and competitiveness
of future procurement and acceptance and inspection of procured items;
and (ii) cause the immediate removal of names of concerned government
officials to the cited government equipment in compliance with the above
cited DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2010-101 and COA Circular No.
2013-004.

We recommended that Management require the:
a. concerned contractor to rectify immediately the deficiencies totaling
₱427,463.96 that were noted in the Improvement and Expansion of
PAGASA Water System (Phase III) and Concreting of Farm to Market
Road projects, and refund the excess contract cost totaling ₱643,421.34
over the COA cost estimates on the two (2) water system projects; and
b. concerned Municipal officials to ensure that the projects are properly
maintained and safeguarded for the intended household beneficiaries to
continuously enjoy the benefits of having safe potable water.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the:
a. Management see to it that development projects proposed to be
undertaken during the budget year are implemented and completed in
the same year;
b. Municipal Engineer prioritize the completion of pending plans and
programs of work for the respective unimplemented projects to facilitate
implementation and completion thereof; and
c. Municipal Development Council require the requesting parties to secure
the necessary documents and requirements in advance, to warrant the
granting of their requested project and avoid delay in the procurement
and implementation process.

We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer submit a
satisfactory justification on the charging of financial assistance granted to
various People’s Organizations against the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that the:
(a) Municipal Engineer submit revised PERT/CPM diagram, together
with the detailed cost of uncompleted portion of the Evacuation Center at
Brgy. Tabon-Tabon as of August 16, 2021, for further evaluation and
computation of liquidated damages, and a written explanation on the
irregular issuance of suspension order; and
(b) Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer,
compute the liquidated damages on delayed completion of the
Evacuation Center at Brgy. Tabon-Tabon project, and deduct the
liquidated damages from any money due or which may become due to
the contractor under the contract, and/or collect such liquidated
damages from the retention money or other securities posted by the
contractor, as the case may be. The Municipal Accountant should
perform circumspect review of all documents in support of
disbursement to prevent the recurrence of undetected delay subject to
liquidated damages.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Accountant submit the lacking Certificates of PhilGEPS
Registration of suppliers and the corresponding BAC Resolutions
recommending the alternative mode of procurement, duly approved by the
Head of the Procuring Entity, and ensure that future transactions of the
same nature are duly substantiated with complete and valid
supporting documents prior to the processing of payments thereof; and
b. BAC Chairman submit a written explanation justifying the resort to
alternative modes of procurement and the splitting of contracts and
ensure that future transactions facilitated through alternative modes of
procurement are in accordance with the standards and procedures
outlined in Annex H of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the:
a. MDRRM and the Municipal Budget Officers provide a satisfactory
justification as to why the appropriated fund for the LDRRM did not
reach the 5% minimum requirement and why the administrative and
operating expenditures of the MDRRMO totaling ₱820,578.60 were
charged against the LDRRMF instead of the regular budget of the
Municipality;
b. MDRRM Officer and other concerned personnel incorporate in the
succeeding LDRRMFIP the unexpended LDRRMF for CY 2019 totaling
₱766,885.49 so that it can be released and utilized to finance the
Municipality’s disaster risk reduction and management programs and
activities;
c. MDRRM Officer refrain from utilizing the LDRRMF to cover the
administrative and operating expenditures of the MDRRMO, instead,
prepare the budget proposal of their office following the guidelines set
under the Local Government Code and Budget Operations Manual for
LGUs and submit the same to the Municipal Mayor for evaluation,
consolidation and inclusion in the forthcoming municipal executive
budget; and
d. Municipal Budget Officer ensure that only expenditures directly related
to disaster risk reduction and management are charged against the
LDRRMF while administrative and operating expenses be charged to the
regular budget of the respective Offices.

We recommended and Management agreed that the GFPS:
a. continue their efforts in promoting and ensuring that gender and
development is mainstreamed in the local policy making, planning,
programming and budgeting; and
b. strictly observe the reporting guidelines under COA Circular No. 2014-
001 dated March 18, 2014 on submission to the Audit Team of the copy of
the GPB within 5 days after the receipt of its approved plan and PCW-
DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016- 01 dated January 12, 2016, with
amendments under PCW Memorandum Circular No. 2020-05 dated
September 11, 2020, on proper attribution of costs of GAD PPAs to be
reported in the GAD Accomplishment Report.

We commended the Offices of the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer for their continuous adherence to pertinent provisions of law in the
withholding and remittance of GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and PhilHealth contributions
and other amounts due to these agencies. However, we would like to give
emphasis on the unidentified reconciling items in the Due to GSIS account
which have been carried forward from prior years. In this regard, we reiterate
our prior year’s recommendations and the Municipal Accountant agreed to
exert utmost efforts to identify the nature and details of reconciling items for
proper remittance or adjustment, as the case may be.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the lacking documents noted in procurement of goods amounting to ₱5,963,864.16, and ensure that all claims against funds of Municipality are supported with complete documents before processing for payment.
observations.managementAction: Management submitted some of the lacking documents.
observations.reason: Non-submission of some of the lacking documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management agreed that the Municipal Budget Officer submit satisfactory explanation to justify the noted unauthorized use of BGCM on subject fuel, oil, and lubricants (FOL) expenses of P38,340.54; and henceforth, ensure strict compliance with the applicable guidelines on the utilization of funds.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant provided a written explanation as to the charging of FOL expenses against BGCM. They were able to justify that the incurrence thereof were used in relation to the LGU’s efforts towards the fight against COVID-19.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents for procurements totaling P12,463,822.50 in compliance with Appendix A, Annex H of Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 01-2020; and the BAC Secretariat and Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents for procurements totaling P12,463,822.50 in compliance with Appendix A, Annex H of Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 01-2020; and BAC Chairman to provide satisfactory justification on the procurement of relief goods totaling P744,550.00 which exceeded the allowable threshold for shopping modality and non-issuance of Notice of Award for subject procurement through Special Repeat Order amounting to P1,935,000.00. Henceforth, adhere to the standards set forth in Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to ensure transparency and accountability in the procurement process; and Municipal Accountant to provide satisfactory explanation on the failure to certify as to funds availability in various Purchase Orders, and ensure that all contracts being entered by the Municipality is supported with certification as to availability of funds and claims are supported with complete and valid documentary requirements before processing the payment.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Secretariat and Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents for procurements totaling P12,463,822.50 in compliance with Appendix A, Annex H of Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 01-2020; The Municipal Accountant to provide satisfactory explanation on the failure to certify as to funds availability in various Purchase Orders, and ensure that all contracts being entered by the Municipality is supported with certification as to availability of funds and claims are supported with complete and valid documentary requirements before processing the payment. The BAC Secretariat submitted the some of the lacking documents. The BAC Secretariat and Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents for procurements totaling P12,463,822.50 in compliance with Appendix A, Annex H of Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 01-2020. The Municipal Accountant to provide satisfactory explanation on the failure to certify as to funds availability in various Purchase Orders, and ensure that all contracts being entered by the Municipality is supported with certification as to availability of funds and claims are supported with complete and valid documentary requirements before processing the payment.
observations.reason: Non-submission of some documentary requirements (Mayor’s Permit, OSS, Income Tax Return, PhilGEPS Registration Certificate, RSBSA Certificate). Moreover, it was noted that the OSS submitted was on a per supplier basis and not on a per transaction basis.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: cause the submission to the Audit Team of the lacking DTRs, and ensure the completeness, accuracy, and propriety of supporting documents before processing payment of future claims; and compute the overpayment of hazard pay for health personnel based on Section 4.5 DBM Budget Circular 2021-1, and require the concerned payees the refund of said overpayment.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant: cause the submission to the Audit Team of the lacking DTRs, and ensure the completeness, accuracy, and propriety of supporting documents before processing payment of future claims; and compute the overpayment of hazard pay for health personnel based on Section 4.5 DBM Budget Circular 2021-1, and require the concerned payees the refund of said overpayment. The Municipal Accountant submitted the lacking DTRs. The Municipal Accountant provided the RHU payroll where deduction of refund from concerned official and employees were reflected.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: The Municipal Accountant: The Municipal Accountant take-up in the books of accounts the appropriate adjusting entries for the book reconciling items amounting to ₱812,606.81, and the reversion of stale checks totaling ₱11,000.00; and the Municipal Treasurer make the necessary representations with the BIR to expedite the processing of the request for refund of amount erroneously remitted, and observe the procedural guidelines on the cancellation and replacement of stale checks as prescribed in Section 59 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant prepared the necessary Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to adjust the identified book reconciling items. The Municipal Treasurer made a follow – up with the BIR – RDO regarding the refund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant, MDRRMO Officer and MSWDO Officer to: examine their records of receipt and distribution of rice, identify the status of unaccounted or missing 511.30 cavans valued at P910,962.76 and submit to the Audit Team a written explanation or report thereon duly supported with relevant documents; and submit to Audit Team the complete RIS and List of Distribution of welfare goods per barangay and individual to substantiate the recorded food supplies and other food supplies expenses as well as the issuance of inventory items. Henceforth, ensure strict compliance with procedural guidelines on the receipt and issuance of inventory items prescribed in Chapter 7 of Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant, MDRRMO Officer and MSWDO Officer to: examine their records of receipt and distribution of rice, identify the status of unaccounted or missing 511.30 cavans valued at P910,962.76 and submit to the Audit Team a written explanation or report thereon duly supported with relevant documents; and submit to Audit Team the complete RIS and List of Distribution of welfare goods per barangay and individual to substantiate the recorded food supplies and other food supplies expenses as well as the issuance of inventory items. Management submitted the RIS and List of Distribution of welfare goods (per barangay and individual). Written explanation, supported with the pertinent distribution list, was submitted to the Audit Team to account for the 511.30 cavans of rice identified in the audit observation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: Municipal Inventory Team conduct on time the physical inventory of all its property, plant and equipment, and prepare and submit the RPCPPE not later than January 31 of the ensuing year in compliance with Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I; Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Inventory Committee (i) reconcile the balances of the PPE per books with the inventory reports, (ii) verify and analyze the discrepancies, and (iii) prepare adjustments to the affected records; and (iii) prepare journal entry voucher to reclassify the cost of air conditioning units of ₱1,501,950.00 from Furniture and Fixtures to appropriate Office Equipment account.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Inventory Team conduct on time the physical inventory of all its property, plant and equipment, and prepare and submit the RPCPPE not later than January 31 of the ensuing year in compliance with Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I; Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Inventory Committee (i) reconcile the balances of the PPE per books with the inventory reports, (ii) verify and analyze the discrepancies, and (iii) prepare adjustments to the affected records; and (iii) prepare journal entry voucher to reclassify the cost of air conditioning units of ₱1,501,950.00 from Furniture and Fixtures to appropriate Office Equipment account. The Municipal Accountant provided the copy of JEV No. 100-21-06 dated June 15, 2021, reflecting the adjusting entry on the reclassification of air-conditioning units from Furniture and Fixtures to Office Equipment.
observations.reason: Incomplete conduct of physical inventory and non-submission of RPCPPE. Non-reconciliation of the accounts due to non-preparation of RPCPPE. The Municipal Accountant only applies depreciation to all recorded PPEs whose physical existence had been determined. Moreover, no action was taken by Management on the non-provision/under-provision of depreciation and reclassification of completed projects under the CIP account to appropriate PPE accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: BAC to submit the lacking evidence of Invitation to three observers, certification on posting of Invitation to Bid at any conspicuous place reserved for this purpose in the premise of the Procuring Entity and Notice of Postponement of bid opening duly posted in the PhilGEPS website, and provide satisfactory justifications on the procedural lapses noted in the procurement; and Municipal Engineer re-inspect the works with noted shallow excavations and institute corrective measures as necessary to prevent unwarranted breakage of pipelines. For future projects, issue the Certificate of Final Acceptance after the one-year Defects Liability Period, and after all the defects and deficiencies, if any, have been corrected.
observations.managementAction: No action taken. The Municipal Engineer reported that they had inspected the project, and the pipes were rehabilitated and 100% completed on June 30, 2021.
observations.reason: Failure to submit justification on and the noted lacking documents on procurement.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management see to it that development projects proposed to be undertaken during the budget year are implemented and completed in the same year; Municipal Engineer prioritize the completion of pending plan and program of works for the respective unimplemented projects to facilitate implementation and completion thereof; and Local Development Council require the requesting parties to secure the necessary documents and requirements in advance, to warrant the granting of their requested project and avoid delay in the procurement and implementation process.
observations.managementAction: Management hired additional technical personnel that will augment their workforce in charge with the implementation of various PPAs under current and continuing 20% Development Fund. Management submitted documents as listed in Part III of the CY2020 AAR.
observations.reason: Optimal utilization of the 20% DF was still not achieved during CY 2021. Non-implementation of certain PPAs is still attributable to the delayed conduct of detailed engineering activities such as preparation of plans and/or POW.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the BAC and Municipal Engineer provide satisfactory explanation to justify the deficiencies noted in the procurement and implementation of subject projects, and submit the lacking Equipment Utilization Schedule to comply with documentary requirements under Revised IRR on RA 9184; and Municipal Engineer see to it that the tarpaulin signboard prescribed in COA Circular No. 2013-004 are posted at the project location of future projects, and the picture displaying tarpaulin signboard are attached to the claims.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant prepared the necessary documents. Copy of the Equipment Utilization Schedule for Construction of Multi-Purpose Hall No. 3 was furnished to the Audit Team. As to the inconsistencies in the date of completion of Multi-Purpose Hall No.1, Management explained that the date of completion stated in the previously submitted Statement of time elapsed was merely erroneous, thus, a revised copy thereof was submitted. Management submitted the lacking tarpaulin signboard for Multi-Purpose Hall No.1 only but they assured the Audit Team that the tarpaulin signboard of Multi-purpose Hall No.3 was also installed and posted within the project site and they merely failed to attach photos of the same.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: BAC Chairman to submit written explanation to justify the regularity of subject procurements through Shopping, refrain from splitting of requisitions, and limit the use of Shopping modality on cases or conditions allowed under Revised IRR of RA 9184 and within the threshold amount of P100,000.00. Henceforth, the BAC should ensure that the Municipality abides by the standard set forth by the Revised IRR of RA 9184 in all its procurement; and Municipal Accountant cause the submission of lacking Certificate of PhilGEPS registration of suppliers and BAC resolution recommending alternative mode of procurement duly approved by HOPE.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Through the Liga ng mga  Barangay encourage active participation of barangay officials to support the solid waste management program of the Municipality by educating their respective constituents through Information Education Campaign (IEC) on the proper segregation of reusable or recyclable materials from compostable wastes and the importance of their participation in the implementation various SWM programs;

b. Through the MENRO, comply with requirements of RA         No. 9003 on waste segregation by                  (a) placing trash bins with proper labels on the designated garbage collection area and other public places and collect only those wastes which are properly segregated, (b) ensure that the existing MRF in Barangay and the LGU’s MRF are functional and the purpose of establishment of MRF is being served, and            (c) provide the necessary support in enjoying the barangays in establishing their own MRF; and 

c. See to it that the operation of the present dumping site complied with Section 2 of             Rule XIII of the IRR of RA No. 9003 until its closure.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Mayor took note of the observations and recommendations and explained the following:

a. Non enforcement of the implementation of mandatory segregation of wastes 

The Municipality is currently implementing a local ordinance wherein every household, institution, and commercial establishment need to segregate their waste. Despite best efforts to inform the people of the ordinance, they still failed to comply with the mandate. Nevertheless, the Municipal Mayor assured to strengthen their efforts to encourage people to segregate their wastes properly through more rigid information dissemination and postings of IEC materials in visible places and coordinate with the barangays to be stringent in monitoring waste segregation in their respective jurisdictions.

b. Non-establishment of MRF in the barangays in the Municipality.

Due to lack of funds, the Municipality failed to establish their MRF in barangays. However, the Municipal Mayor assured to exemplify their efforts in looking for financial assistance to help the barangays establish their MRF as designed and prepared by the Municipality.

c. Failure to meet the requirements in operating dumpsite.

The Municipality issued notice to the public that the dumpsite used in Bangkatan was already closed pursuant to Article 6, Section 77 of RA No. 9003. The garbage seen during the inspection were brought there due to the recent Typhoon Usman where majority of the town was flooded and no other location is available to put the garbage on. However, the best efforts were exerted through proposal to the Environment Management Bureau (EMB) MIMAROPA for the safe closure and rehabilitation of the plan of their disposal facility. Also, they are still on the process of securing loan from the Land Bank of the Philippines to establish a functional disposal facility and procure effective machineries to achieve ecological solid waste management.

In addition, the Municipal Mayor pointed out that the designated MENRO is concurrently the Municipal Assessor hence, handling both posts become an extremely difficult task where lapses are inevitable.
observations.reason: The Safe closure and rehabilitation plan for open  dumpsite was pending for approval.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and invited attention to the following responsibilities: a. The Municipal Inventory Team (i) conduct an inventory of the local roads under the Municipal Government’s jurisdiction, (ii) prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System, and (iii) reconcile the list of inventory per local road inventory report and road map with those recorded in the Registry of Public Infrastructure; c. The Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects identified, and provide report on the cost and status of on-going projects and submit detailed cost of components of infrastructure projects; d. The Municipal Accountant (i) analyze the Registry of Public Infrastructure on Roads and other Public Infrastructure accounts, and identify the accounts to be recorded back in the books of accounts, (ii) prepare the Journal Voucher (JV) to record the beginning balance of the local road network and its components in the general ledger and the Local Road Network Ledger Card, respectively, (iii) support the JV with the Registry of Public Infrastructures for Roads, working paper on the distribution of costs for the road components, and working paper on the determination of the depreciated replacement cost for the road components with no available cost per registry, (iv) keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network, and (v) prepare a lapsing schedule for the computation of the depreciation for each component at the end of the year; and e. The Municipal Budget Officer provide the updated Registries of Appropriation, Allotment and Obligations, and furnish copies of the approved budget, supplemental budgets, realignment and distribution of the lump sum appropriation and the corresponding legislative approval.
